The problem requires determining the maximum width across all levels, so a Level Order Traversal (BFS) is highly suitable.
To account for 'null' nodes efficiently, assign a unique index to each node. For a node at index `i`, its left child will be at index `2 * i` and its right child at `2 * i + 1`.
The width of a level is simply `(index of last non-null node on level) - (index of first non-null node on level) + 1`.

Beware of Integer Overflow. The indices `2 * i` can quickly become very large. In languages like JS, normalize indices at the start of each level (subtract the first node's index from all subsequent nodes) or use BigInt.
